speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four of an interview with Ester Lebensold. Today is November 27, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Montreal, are you a member of any organizations, particularly survivor groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I belong to the Survivors, and I belong to the [INAUDIBLE]. My husband is a member. And to the Lodz Society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there many people from Lodz living in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, yes. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the society do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The society, we-- we try to have money for Israel. We are making money for Israel. And campaign-- we are campaigning for Israel. We make [? phone ?] drives, and we do a lot. We have a lot of things in Israel what we give money for-- hospitals, for special rooms, and a lot. A lot. I have a calendar and it's [? marking ?] out what we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Does the Lodz Society, is that a memorial group? Do you have a memorial for the people who died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have-- we have a cemetery, and we have a big-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how you call it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the Lodz, you get the big monument. And in the back, we have from my parents, from my brother, we have medals. It marks the names from them. So every year before the high holidays, we have Yizkor with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there are other people who are able to be with you there who went through the ghetto with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All-- a very lot-- a lot of people, they came to the-- to the Yizkor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had mentioned to me when we were talking that the-- the experiences people had during the war, some of them it made them more religious, and some of them it made them less religious. How did your experiences affect you and your family this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- My brother got very religious. My sister is very religious. She-- And what I saw, what I saw-- I passed through a hospital, and I see they have the babies, the newborn babies, and they stick to the wall, and everything gets splashed-- the blood, everything, is-- where my mother-in-law used to live, it was a big hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I have to pass this hospital and I saw it, and I start to-- to believe in nothing-- in nothing. A child was born an hour ago, or yesterday. What the child has done? This is the-- that's a shit-- excuse me. That's scheisse. Boom.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it. My eyes saw it. From that time, I can't believe. My parents, they were very religious. I came from a very religious home. In Poland, mostly, 99% people was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you feel you're one of those people who it changed your beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot-- Did the opposite. My brother believes, because he came out alive. He says there is a miracle. There is a God. And I saw what they have-- what have done with the babies. I'm sorry, I can't. It's hard-- it's hard for me to believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you wanted to do this interview while you were visiting your sister in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that you could get it done. Is there anything else you'd like to add, any-- now that it's been 50 years since the war, any other thoughts that you may have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lodz, they make the-- in-- in the library by us, the liquidation from the Lodz ghetto. It was a beautiful evening. And they give me to light a candle. And it was speeches, and it was unbelievable. It was beautiful. They got a lot of speakers. And they got a thousand-- a few thousand people. That's the Jewish library. [INAUDIBLE] everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Montreal.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montreal, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I think it's the Bronfman's building, across the street, the Bronfman's Theatre. They're from the-- you know the Bronfmans, you hear from them. That's the-- they have the Sigram-- Sigram?

speaker: interviewer
text: Seagram's.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seagram's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're-- they're helpful to your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: To our group, no, but they have a museum and their name, and her mother, Mrs. Bronfman, she just died of-- two months ago. She was 92 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you so much for this interview today. You certainly had a lot of information to share about the Lodz ghetto and your experiences in Bremen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. And that's my first picture after the war. I make them in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this? 1945? Shortly after?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- yeah, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. That's my daughter. She was born in Israel, and we came to Montreal, 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I know. They send it from Winnipeg. I have no idea. The picture's not made in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a fairly recent picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: A fairly recent picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The recent picture is from the wedding. Picture's maybe one year old. [INAUDIBLE] it's me and my husband and my granddaughter. It's made in Maimonides. That's the home where he's staying, called Maimonides.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago, because this summer she got married. She wasn't able to come. It was the summer before. It was a surprise. That's my grandson, James. He's-- in this picture is maybe 16 years. Now he's going to be almost 20 in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's my daughter, my son-in-law, my granddaughter's wedding. When was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding-- in last-- this summer, on the 25th of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your son-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your granddaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter Sheila. And my daughter's name is Fruma. That's my granddaughter and her groom. And his name is DJ, and her name is Sheila. That's the wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They get married in-- in Carman, Manitoba.
